Temporal Trends in RSV Wastewater Concentrations Across Vermont NWSS Monitoring Sites, 2023-26

Jacob Pancoast | University of Vermont
B.S. Candidate, Computer Science & Epidemiology Minor

June 10, 2026

Abstract

In the United States, RSV seasonality has been disrupted following the COVID-19 pandemic. Understanding changes trends may require new analysis methods in areas with gaps in clinical coverage. Wastewater-based epidemiology (WBE) is a cost-effective and rapid tool which can be used to analyze and assess the burden of diseases through wastewater collection. Based on historical trends, expert opinion, and scenario modeling, the CDC expected the 2025-26 RSV season to be no more severe than usual. As of late April, 2026, RSV-NET surveillance indicated RSV levels were high in Vermont. Due to low population, Vermont is a state that may be misrepresented by national predictions.

In 2023, participating Vermont NWSS sites began monitoring RSV levels. Since 2023, 6 sites have data for at least 1 season. Data obtained from data.cdc.gov was used to compare trends across sites to infer the burden of RSV in Vermont this season.

Only 3 of the 6 participating Vermont NWSS sites contained enough data for analysis (\(\ge 3\) years). For every remaining site, each was found to have an increased trend in RSV for 2026 using STL decomposition and fitting a generalized additive model. Analyzing season onsets and offsets showed this season also started much later than usual, which may be due to climate change or variation in viral shedding from who was infected and how they were infected.

A large percentage of RSV burden is made up of young children and infants, who receive nirsevimab treatment at the beginning of the respiratory virus season. The delayed and prolonged RSV season in Vermont this year may reflect expiring immunity. Vermont’s wastewater data should be compared against clinical data to investigate correlations between the two, which may provide more insight into infection demographics . Better understanding how RSV concentrations in wastewater represent the burden of disease in a community should influence public health decision making and vaccine schedule recommendations.

Introduction

Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV)

Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is an RNA virus belonging to the Pneumoviridae family of viruses. RSV infection is most often accompanied by difficulty breathing, coughing, sneezing, congestion, and decreased appetite, but may lead to more severe illnesses including bronchiolitis and pneumonia in high-risk groups including infants, young children, and older adults1. In the United States, RSV activity peaks during respiratory virus season. Typically, cases increase in the fall, peak around December or January, and decrease into the spring1,2.

Clinically, RSV presents similarly to the common cold, influenza, and COVID-19. As a result, RSV infection is frequently misdiagnosed, especially in high risk populations3. Worldwide, RSV is a leading cause of acute lower respiratory infections among both children and elderly. In the United States alone, RSV was estimated to cause between 60,000-160,000 hospitalizations as well as 6,000-10,000 deaths annually among adults aged 65 and over in 20234.

Figure 1. Estimated ranges of annual flu and RSV burdens. Figure 1A was obtained from the CDC and shows flu burden from 2010-255. Figure 1B was created using data from a CDC MMWR report on RSV from 2023 estimating the annual burden.

The general hospitalization rate for RSV may be between 2 - 4.4 times lower than influenza, while the mortality rate may be between 1.05 - 5.2 times lower than influenza (Figure 1). Among elderly populations, RSV has been shown to exhibit higher rates of pneumonia, hospitalization, and extended hospital stays compared to the flu6. Lowering the incidence of RSV for these at-risk groups is critical to improve health outcomes and reduce costs associated with prolonged hospital stays.

RSV Monitoring Systems

The RSV-Hospitalization Surveillance Network (RSV-NET) is a CDC-led program that monitors laboratory-confirmed RSV hospitalizations to estimate disease burden and identify trends in virus circulation7. Established in 2018, this program serves 14 states (found here) and over 170 counties. Most states do not yet participating in RSV-NET, which limits centralized monitoring.

Most states have labs participating in the National Respiratory and Eneteric Virus Surveillance System (NREVSS), which measure weekly positive RSV tests. Low-population states such as Vermont are undercovered due to the presence of fewer NREVSS labs.

Gaps in clinical surveillance may be supplemented by wastewater surveillance. The CDC collects wastewater data reported by state and local health departments through the National Wastewater Surveillance System (NWSS)8.

Wastewater based epidemiology (WBE) is an important tool in assessing respiratory viral disease burden 2,9-11 that has gained traction as an epidemiological method since the COVID-19 pandemic12 despite being in practice for over 80 years13. While clinical surveillance remains valuable in populations with high access to testing14, WBE offers advantages in that it is not subject to the same delays and constraints as clinical testing 2,9,11; it is also less invasive, cost effective11, and may be generalizable to neighboring communities2. Without the reliance on testing access, WBE may better indicate viral disease burden 2,9,10 in lower-income communities while also accounting for asymptomatic individuals, which may represent around 17% of overall cases15.

As of 2022, RSV testing was generally not recommended for children in Vermont since it does not significantly alter treatment outcomes16. Because publicly available RSV testing and hospitalization data are scarce for Vermont, WBE is a critical tool in understanding RSV epidemiology in the state.

Figure 2. United States NWSS Sites reporting RSV concentrations as of May 14, 2026. Source: CDC Wastewater Monitoring Program17.

Sites across the country (Figure 2) are selected by local governments, based on site effectiveness and population coverage. Many health departments prioritize enrolling sites with a high social vulnerability index. 89% of participating NWSS sites are located at a wastewater treatment plant17. In Vermont, the number of NWSS-participating wastewater treatment sites is low (n = 6) compared to states with similar population density (Minnesota, n = 29; West Virginia, n = 17)18. Undercoverage in Vermont may affect surveillance and health outcomes.

Wastewater collection differs between sites. Some sites may sample untreated wastewater (sewage and stormwater) or primary sludge (suspended solids from wastewater). Sampling methods also differ, including grab samples (from a point in time) or composite samples (aggregated grab samples). Sampling frequency also differs between sites17. For each sample, various metrics are recorded including flow rate, virus type, viral quantification, and contamination measurements. Each site is also associated with a region and population served8,17. Viral quantification is measured using repeated PCR tests, and all data is submitted to the CDC.

RSV in Vermont

Figure 3. Percentage of positive RSV tests by state as of the week ending April 25, 2026. Source: CDC NREVSS Dashboard18.

In the 2025-26 season, the CDC identified RSV activity started later in most parts of the US, and also peaked later19. As of late April 2026, Vermont had one of the highest proportions of positive RSV tests in the country (Figure 3). This report investigates temporal trends across Vermont’s six NWSS monitoring sites from 2023-26 to assess the burden of the 2025-26 RSV season to evaluate how well the CDC’s assessment of the 2025-26 season20 generalizes to Vermont and discuss Vermont-specific response to RSV.

Methods

CDC wastewater data for RSV was obtained from Data.CDC.gov. Each row represents a wastewater sample for RSV from participating NWSS sites, including dates of collection21. The “pcr_target_avg_conc” column was chosen for analysis of RSV concentrations rather than “pcr_target_flowpop_lin” since the flow-population-normalized column had large amounts of missing data. Population differences between sites were small enough to accommodate this decision (Table S1). Before analysis, the data was cleaned and visualized. During this step, a seven-day rolling average concentration was calculated for each data point to reduce noise and artifacts. This average was used for all further analysis.

All data cleaning, analyses, and visualizations were conducted using R (version 4.5.2; R Foundation).

Site Comparison

Each sample was classified by season (starting in the second half of one year, through the first half of the following year). Respiratory virus season usually lasts October through May, but a broader range was used to observe any changes in seasonality for the 2025-26 season. Without benchmark clinical indicators to define seasonality14,22, season onset and offset were defined using the methodology for WBE outlined in Boehm et. al2,9,23. For each individual site, season onset was defined as the first day in the second half of the year where 100% of samples collected in a 14 day look back period had an RSV concentration equal to or above 2 times the limit of detection for that site. Season offset was defined as the first day following a season onset date where 50% or less of the samples collected in a 14 day lookback had concentrations above or equal to 2 times the limit of detection for that site. This method was originally described for sites that measure viral concentration in copies per dry gram of sludge. For this analysis, this definition was extended to sites measuring copies per liter of wastewater. This was a feasible extrapolation since the threshold proportion was preserved by using the individual limit of detection for each site.

From this data, tables were constructed to compare season lengths, peak days, peak years for each site, and season onsets and offsets.

Peak Comparison

Peaks analysis was performed to identify per-site and per-season peaks across the data.

Additional temporal information was added to each row, including “days elapsed since season onset”, and “days elapsed since last anchor date”. To compare offsets in seasonality, June 1st was selected as the anchor date since it never fell in-season. From this data, graphs comparing seasonal peaks were constructed.

Seasonal Decomposition

For all further analysis, only the three sites containing consistent data spanning \(n \ge 3\) years were used (sites 1965, 1966, and 1975) due to inadequate coverage in the remaining sites.

Seasonal-Trend decomposition using Loess (STL) decomposes a time series (\(y_t\)) into trend-cycle (\(T_t\)), seasonal (\(S_t\)), and remainder (\(R_t\)) components such that \(y_t\) = \(T_t\) + \(S_t\) + \(R_t\). Classical decomposition may also be multiplicative, however STL only supports additive decomposition. While proportional variation in the magnitude of seasonal fluctuations over the time series suggested a multiplicative decomposition, concentrations were log transformed to convert the multiplicative structure to additive, while also normalizing the right skew in the data.

Concentrations were then grouped by week and averaged to create the regular weekly intervals required for decomposition. Data gaps in site 1966 were filled with concentration = 0 for the weeks of April 21, 2024; April 28, 2024; and May 5; 2024. After decomposition, graphs of each component per site were created to compare trends across sites.

Generalized Additive Model (GAM)

STL decomposition identified that long-term trend and seasonality were the dominant sources of variation in Vermont’s RSV wastewater concentration, informing predictor selection for a generalized additive model (GAM). GAMs model the expected value of an outcome as the sum of smooth, independent functions for each predictor. GAMs are highly interpretable and can flexibly model non-linear relationships.

Data was filtered and grouped by each relevant site. For each sample, four columns were added: the first collection date for the site; days elapsed since that date; the log-transformed rolling 7-day average; and the day of the year.

GAM models were fit to a Gaussian distribution using the mgcv24 package. The predictors—days elapsed and day of the year—were both fit as a smoothed curve to explain the weekly mean log RSV concentration. “Days elapsed” was based in a thin plate regression spline, which determines knot locations based on the data. “Day of the year” was based in a cyclic cubic spline to account for seasonal trends. REML smoothing parameter estimation was used to reduce the likelihood of oversmoothing the data.

The significance of each smooth term was assessed using approximate p-values from the model summary.

Results

An initial overview of the data sought to visualize early patterns to note trend and seasonal observations, compare sites, and identify any initial limitations of further analysis.

Figure 4. Exploratory data analysis of RSV wastewater concentrations, faceted by NWSS collection site. Colored lines represent the calculated seven-day rolling average. Note: per CDC guidance, RSV Concentrations should not be compared between sites due to different sample collection methods21; Vermont, 2023-26.

Exploratory visualizations revealed clear seasonal patterns across sites consistent with known RSV epidemiology. Concentrations generally begin to increase in the fall and peak typically in January or February before declining again in April to May. This pattern was consistent across all sites. Only sites 1965, 1966, and 1975 contained three full seasons worth of data, providing enough data for further statistical tests (seasonal decomposition and GAM).

Season Info

The following tables summarize seasonal characteristics across Vermont’s NWSS monitoring sites, including season lengths, season peaks, peak years per site, and season onset and offset dates. As of May 19, 2026, the 2025-26 season was still ongoing at sites 1965, 1966, 1975 in Vermont.

Season lengths (Table 1)

Table 1. Season lengths by site, including the averaged length.
Site 2022-23 2023-24 2024-25 2025-26 Avg. / site (all years) Avg. / site (excl. 2022-23)
1965 47 133 122 139 110.25 126.06
1966 14 159 119 126 104.5 127.12
1967 — — — 42 42 42
1975 56 164 203 126 137.25 157.56
1976 — 7 — — 7 7
2350 — — 19 — 19 19
Avg. / season (all sites) 39 115.75 115.75 108.25 70 79.79
Avg. / season (excl. low-data sites) 39 152 148 130.33 117.33 136.92

Season lengths for 2022-23 are inaccurate due to large gaps in data for that season; data collection did not begin until late into the season’s latter half. Sites 1967, 1976, 2350 have extremely low coverage—these sites were not used for further analysis due to insufficient data to meet the onset and offset detection threshold. Site 1975 had the longest seasons recorded, and season lengths were generally similar between sites 1965, 1966, and 1975. Across seasons, lengths were also relatively similar on average, excluding the ongoing 2025-26 and undercovered 2022-23 seasons.

Season peaks (Table 2)

Table 2. Season peak days by site, including the average peak by season and site.
Site 2022-23 2023-24 2024-25 2025-26 Avg. / site
1965 2023-03-03 2024-01-12 2025-01-30 2026-02-23 02-09
1966 2023-03-21 2024-02-22 2025-01-30 2026-03-31 03-05
1967 — — — 2026-03-04 03-05
1975 2023-03-07 2024-02-22 2025-02-18 2026-04-06 03-08
1976 — 2024-02-20 — — 02-21
2350 — — 2025-02-13 — 02-14
Avg. / season 2023-03-10 2024-02-11 2025-02-07 2026-03-16 02-26

Seasonal peaks most commonly fall in early to mid-February. In 2025-26, the peak fell a month later in mid-March.

Peak years (Table 3)

Table 3. Peak year for each site.
Site Year
1965 2023-2024
1966 2023-2024
1967 2025-2026
1975 2025-2026
1976 2023-2024
2350 2024-2025

The most common year with the highest peak for each site was 2023-24, followed by 2025-26. Site 1967’s result is trivial since the site is limited to one year of data.

Season onsets and offsets (Table 4)

Table 4. Average season onset and offset each year.
Season Average onset Average offset
2022_2023 Low data 2023-04-18
2023_2024 2023-12-21 2024-04-15
2024_2025 2024-12-31 2025-04-26
2025_2026 2026-01-13 Ongoing

The 2022-23 season did not have enough data to attribute an onset date. The 2023-24 and 2024-25 seasons both began in mid to late December and ended in mid to late April. The 2022-23 season also offset in mid to late April, which may suggest greater data coverage would indicate season onset was similar to the following two seasons. The 2025-26 season began 2-3 weeks later in mid-January and was still ongoing as of May 19, 2026.

STL Decomposition and GAM

Figure 5. RSV wastewater concentrations in Vermont by day since the start of the on-season; Vermont, 2023-26

Sites 1965, 1966, and 1975 all showed high spikes in RSV concentration during mid-late season 2023-24. RSV concentrations were highest overall in the 2023-24 season in Chittenden county, while the 2025-26 season concentrations were the highest in Washington county (Figure 5).

Figure 6. RSV wastewater concentrations in Vermont by day since June 1st of the previous off-season; Vermont, 2023-26

Generally, seasons occurring earlier in the data collection periods exhibit earlier season onset and peak earlier. The 2023-24 season rises and peaks early across all sites before the 2024-25 and 2025-26 seasons rise. Peaks in the latter seasons appear to be between 30-50 days later than peaks in the 2023-24 season (Figure 6).

Figure 7. STL decomposition of log weekly RSV wastewater concentration over the observation period at NWSS site 1965; Chittenden County, Vermont, 2023-26

Seasonal decomposition showed seasonal peaks occur on average on 01-26, 01-09, and 02-16 for sites 1965, 1966, and 1975 respectively. Site 1965 showed a dip in the overall trend of the data during summer 2024. From there the trend continues to increase steadily throughout 2025 and early 2026, where it is currently highest (Figure 7). Site 1966 peaked overall during the 2023-24 respiratory virus season and steadily decreased until the start of the 2025-26 season. The trend for site 1975 fluctuates slightly over time, but shows an increasing trend in the 2025-26 season (Figure S1). All sites showed a clear seasonal cycle alongside a non-linear long-term trend.

Table 5. GAM model summary statistics for sites 1965, 1966, and 1975. All smooth terms are significant at p < 0.001.
Site Trend p-value Seasonality p-value Deviance explained EDF for trend EDF for seasonality
1965 <0.001 <0.001 70.0% 6.64 6.42
1966 <0.001 <0.001 75.0% 6.40 6.71
1975 <0.001 <0.001 78.0% 7.27 6.10

GAM identified statistically significant long-term trends and seasonal smooth terms at sites 1965, 1966, and 1975 (all p < 2e-16, or p \(\approx\) 0), with average degrees of freedom of 6.77 and 6.41 for trend and seasonality, respectively. Site 1975 had the highest deviance explained, followed by 1966, and 1965 (Table 5).

Figure 8. Generalized additive model (GAM) smoothed components for RSV wastewater concentration over the observation period at NWSS site 1965; Chittenden County, Vermont, 2023-26

The partial effect of the ‘days elapsed’ component for site 1965’s GAM shows a trend with relative peaks around October 19, 2023 and April 01, 2025. In the 2025-26 season, the data increases towards an absolute maximum as of May 19, 2026. The seasonal component shows a peak in RSV concentrations within the first fifty days of the year, with a sharp decrease that reaches a minimum around mid-July. Concentrations begin to rise again gradually, before sharply increasing beginning in late October.

During off-season periods, residual plotting revealed patterns of large negative residuals indicating model overprediction of near-zero values. These periods were also accompanied by wider confidence intervals, corroborating the model’s limitations in predicting near-zero concentrations. During on-season periods, concentration spikes occasionally exceeded the model’s prediction. Similar patterns in trend were observed for sites 1966 and 1975 (Figure S1, S2, S3).

Limitations

The CDC advised against comparison of RSV concentrations across sites due to differences in collection methods, collection frequencies, and laboratory testing methods13. Exploratory analysis found that sites 1967, 1976, and 2350 had large gaps in data that affected seasonal classification using the method defined in Boehm et al.2,9,23 These sites were not analyzed further using STL decomposition and GAM due to low coverage. The 2022-23 season was also excluded due to low coverage. The first date on record for any site was March 03, 2023—3 months into a typical season, which undermined the validity of onset date for the 2022-23 season.

In both component graphs of the GAM, large negative residuals in the off-season (around May 01 to November 10) reflect systematic overprediction of near-zero values by the model—this is a known limitation of Gaussian family distributions in epidemic modeling25. Towards the beginning and end of each off-season, the magnitude of the model’s overprediction is greatest since it expects a continuous distribution. Future analyses may benefit from zero-inflated or hurdle model frameworks25, which are frequently used in epidemic analysis pipelines due to their ability to account for excess zeros in the data.

Clinical data for RSV hospitalizations were not available for Vermont, precluding validation of wastewater trends against clinical outcomes. Wastewater viral concentrations and clinical outcomes have been correlated in previous studies9,11; hospitalization data could cross-validate these findings and strengthen interpretation of wastewater trends as indicators of community disease burden.

Discussion

For the 2025-26 season, the CDC indicated that the national RSV burden was not likely to be more severe compared to previous seasons12. However, Vermont only represents about 0.2% of the US population; an increase in the state’s RSV burden would contribute minimally to national aggregated data. This could obscure critical state-level variation for low-population states in national surveillance reports.

Excluding low-coverage sites 1967, 1976, and 2350, only site 1975 showed (Figure 5) an absolute peak for the 2025-26 season (Table 3). Interestingly, while seasonal decomposition showed only site 1965 had an increasing trend in the 2025-26 season (Figure 7, S1), GAM showed sites 1965, 1966, and 1975 all climbed towards absolute maxima in the ‘days elapsed’ component (Figure 8, S2, S3), indicating higher RSV wastewater concentrations in the 2025-26 season. The smoothing splines approach in GAM better detected trends that LOESS local smoothing underweighted.

While RSV wastewater concentrations in the 2025-26 season were elevated relative to prior years, this cannot be interpreted as increased disease burden without considering viral shedding dynamics. Wastewater viral concentrations reflect the volume shed by individuals in a community 9,10,26, which has been found to vary by age, symptom severity, and infection status 27,28. Wastewater concentrations may have been higher this season due to variation in viral shedding based on who was infected and the conditions under which transmission occurred.

RSV season begins in fall and peaks in December or January 1,2, lasting 3-5 months on average in temperate regions 29,30 such as Vermont. Vermont’s WBE data indicated seasons lasted 4.56 months on average from 2023-26 (Table 1), began in mid-late December, ended in mid-late April (Table 4), and peaked in January or February (Table 2). The 2025-26 season strayed from these trends; it began later, peaked in late February to early April, and was still ongoing as of May 19, 2026 (Figure 7-8, S1-S3). Discrepancies in season duration have been found to be important in inferring the burden of disease and allocating resources in a community2.

Recent winters have been unpredictable in onset and severity of conditions31. Notably, the 2025-26 winter had a delayed onset and was especially cold, which may influence the spread of RSV. Generally, inclement climates increase exposure by forcing people together inside 29. Dry, cold winters are known to encourage the stability and transmission of airborne viruses, including RSV, as water evaporates from respiratory particles, decreasing volume and increasing aerosolization32,33,34,35. RSV survives longer on fomites in these conditions, which are a primary source of infection33,36. In humans, cold air inhalation also limits metabolic functions that assist in clearing mucus and the activity of innate immune cells32.

RSV transmission was reduced in 2020 following nationwide nonpharmaceutical COVID-19 interventions 37,38. These changes also disrupted subsequent seasons. In 2021-22, RSV season began earlier and lasted longer. In 2022-23, the season also began earlier 22,38. These recent temporal shifts may predict a return to pre-pandemic seasonality as population immunity and viral circulation adapt and reach a new equilibrium. A late winter could disrupt the change in this equilibrium, potentially contributing to Vermont’s higher RSV wastewater concentrations in 2025-26.

Viral shedding decreases with age28, however infants protected through vaccination or protective medicines such as nirsevimab have lower risks of infection and severity of symptoms; immunized infants shed less virus. Vaccination data in Vermont indicated infants made up significantly less of the vulnerable population and were more likely to be vaccinated for RSV than older adults2.

Nirsevimab is a monoclonal antibody that protects infants for around 5 months39,40. This medicine is most frequently administered to infants younger than 8 months, before their first RSV season — usually in October. Late peaks in the 2025-26 season often occurred in late February or later (Table 2, Figure 6). Considering infected infants shed more RSV than older adults, these late peaks would coincide with nirsevimab immunity expiring 5 months after initial dosing in October 2025.

All PCR data collected from Vermont’s NWSS sites was aggregated for RSV-A and RSV-B. RSV-A is known to have higher rates of shedding in infected individuals25. Further analysis could provide insight to geographic and temporal changes in the prevalence of each strain to determine if higher prevalence of RSV-A influenced increased wastewater concentrations.

Implications for Public Health Practice

Annual state guidelines could provide recommendations for the timing of seasonal nirsevimab doses for infants based on the start of the respiratory virus season. Providing the protective medicine in November or early December could provide better coverage during the RSV season (Table 2 and 4). This approach may be essential to help manage limited resources while maintaining infant immunity for as long as possible during relevant times. Dosing is only recommended once a season41, however, careful consideration of repeat dosing protocols for extended seasons may be warranted.

As of May 2026, no national eligibility criteria exist for access to RSV testing9. This gap is exacerbated by existing barriers to healthcare for at-risk populations9. Centralized eligibility criteria based on age, symptoms, and risk-group may lower testing barriers for patients. Vermont may benefit from centralized criteria in-state to reduce barriers to timely diagnosis for providers and at-risk patients.

Furthermore, access to hospitalization data would be critical to understand clinical implications of WBE. Prior studies have found correlation between WBE concentration peaks and hospitalization rates9, and that delayed wastewater peaks relative to hospitalization rates may reflect infected individuals not seeking care10. This is especially important to consider in rural communities, where Vermont residents have been found to have travel times up to 3.6 times longer to a healthcare facility26. Understanding the correlation between WBE and clinical outcomes may allow public health officials to better infer the burden of disease and allocate limited resources such as vaccines and tests30 while also identifying gaps in healthcare access for at-risk populations.

Generalizability is a recognized strength of WBE2, however Vermont does not have enough participating NWSS sites to adequately monitor RSV burden across all of Vermont’s rural counties. Increasing the number of participating NWSS sites in Vermont would allow better generalization to underrepresented rural communities that are far from current state NWSS sites. Implementation of strain-specific surveillance at each monitoring site would enable correlation analyses between RSV strain prevalence, wastewater concentrations, and disease burden.

Conclusion

This analysis identified elevated RSV wastewater concentrations and a delayed 2025-26 season across Vermont NWSS monitoring sites. The 2025-26 season was still ongoing as of May 19, 2026, and continued monitoring is warranted to fully characterize the season’s trajectory. Expanding Vermont’s wastewater surveillance infrastructure and integrating clinical outcome data into public databases would substantially improve the state’s capacity to monitor and respond to RSV in future seasons as RSV seasonality returns to pre-pandemic patterns. Annual seasonality data should inform public health guidelines for immunization timelines and testing protocols.
